game_name: Quest for Glory: So You Want to Be a Hero

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Determines equipment, puzzle-solving methods, available quests throughout game
NPCs provide quests, shops offer equipment/potions, establishes open-ended exploration pattern
Basic combat encounters, progression toward main antagonist revelation
Non-human enemy type, increases combat variety
Optional but reward-yielding activities, build character stats through usage-based progression
Non-combat NPCs, story world-building, social interaction elements
Magic ability scales with spell usage; intelligence grows through repeated casting
Environmental changes affect NPC availability, scenery, quest opportunities; time pressure element
Resource management layer; running/fighting cause fatigue requiring rest
Earned money from quest completion purchases gear and consumables

element_introduction_order.element: Recurring Characters (Erasmus, Fenrus, References to Erana)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Series continuity, world-building, hints at broader Quest for Glory universe
Central curse source; confrontation is optimal ending path
Main quest objective; freeing baronet is part of optimal ending

element_introduction_order.element: Brigand Leader Identity Revelation (Elsa von Spielburg)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Plot twist integrating combat/quest objectives; her curse must be broken for optimal ending

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Open-ended exploration with natural tension-relief cycles driven by resource management (hunger, fatigue, equipment) and time-of-day mechanics; combat encounters interrupt exploration phases; optional quests provide pacing flexibility

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Side quests are entirely optional; completion order player-determined; secondary quest solutions vary by class (Thief/Fighter/Magic User paths through same world)
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Folklore-sourced world with hidden fantasy creatures (dryad, hermit, Meeps) discoverable through exploration; lore about Erana distributed through NPC dialogue

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial-like pacing via class selection; basic brigand encounters introduce combat; survival mechanics (eating, sleeping) establish resource awareness
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Kobolds and increased enemy variety; side quests build stats through usage-based progression; day/night cycle complexity increases; fantasy creatures introduce narrative depth
difficulty_curve.late_game: Baba Yaga and curse mechanics introduce complex plot threads; Elsa von Spielburg identity twist; optimal ending path requires multiple curse-breaking objectives; skill mastery from accumulated play becomes critical
notable_spikes: Encountering Baba Yaga curse effects (narrative/difficulty shift)
Final confrontation with main antagonist and curse removal puzzles

notable_level_patterns.description: Town serves as central safe hub with multiple quest lines radiating outward into wilderness; players return to town between expeditions for rest, shopping, quest updates
Same geographic space contains different solvable quests per character class; Thief encounters lockpicking puzzles, Magic User seeks spell ingredients, Fighter pursues combat encounters
Day/night cycle creates temporary content unavailability; NPCs have schedules; enemy types/behaviors differ by time of day; forces temporal planning and revisitation

Character abilities unlock through action frequency rather than level grinding; using magic increases Magic ability; fighting increases Strength; stat growth gates access to certain quests and solutions
Fantasy creatures (dryad, hermit, Meeps) require exploration to discover; not required for completion but reward story depth and world-building
